name: Eddie Lang
abstract: Eddie Lang (born Salvatore Massaro; October 25, 1902 – March 26, 1933) was an American musician who is credited as the father of jazz guitar. During the 1920s, he gave the guitar a prominence it previously lacked as a solo instrument, as part of a band or orchestra, and as accompaniment for vocalists.

about_quotes.speaker: Keith Ingham
about_quotes.evidence: Dave liked that particularly, the wonderful players like Eddie Lang and Bix Beiderbecke... And I got to hear them on records
about_quotes.doc_title: Keith Ingham interviewed by Monk Rowe, Chautauqua, New York, September 12, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: John Abercrombie
about_quotes.evidence: I don’t know his playing that well but I remember kind of like the father of modern kind of guitar.
about_quotes.doc_title: John Abercrombie interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, April 19, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Stanley Dance
about_quotes.evidence: The influence in England to begin with was mainly coming from, well very often came from New York musicians who were playing in The Savoy Hotel and places like that. They came over and they were full of we’ll say Red Nichols, Joe Venuti, Ed Lang and people like that.
about_quotes.doc_title: Stanley Dance interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Diego, California, February 12, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Laurence Juber
about_quotes.evidence: Perry Botkin really, I think, and I’m kind of learning this from talking to Perry Jr. and from just doing my own research that Perry was kind of the link between the New York jazz studio guitar players, like Eddie Lang, and the L.A. studio players.
about_quotes.doc_title: Laurence Juber interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, July 21, 2020 [interview 2 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Ray Bauduc
about_quotes.evidence: Jimmy was playing, uh, at one time with Joe Benuti and, and, and Eddie Lang and those guys in the, in the pit for the, uh, oh, what the, that was the name of the show.

about_quotes.speaker: Leslie Gourse
about_quotes.evidence: there are others, Eddie Lang and Oscar Moore in the older crowd. So I mean by now there is a certain amount I’ve picked up enough to know who were the major influences
about_quotes.doc_title: Leslie Gourse interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, March 9, 1996
